name: Ryugu-jo
description: Ryugu-jo, the Dragon Palace, is a magnificent underwater palace in Japanese mythology, said to be located at the bottom of the sea. It is the dwelling of Ryujin, the dragon god of the sea, and his family. Ryugu-jo is often depicted as a lavish and opulent palace, constructed from coral, crystal, and other precious materials. Time flows differently within its walls, and those who visit may experience a significant discrepancy between the time spent inside and the time passed in the outside world. The palace is a place of immense power and wonder, holding treasures and secrets beyond mortal comprehension. It is a symbol of the vast and mysterious depths of the ocean and the power of the dragon gods who rule over them. Ryugu-jo plays a crucial role in several Japanese folktales, often serving as a destination for heroes and mortals who seek knowledge, power, or healing. The palace is guarded by loyal sea creatures and is often protected by magical barriers, making it a challenging place to reach. Its existence represents the connection between the human world and the realm of the gods, highlighting the importance of respecting and understanding the natural world. Those who are invited or deemed worthy may be granted access to its wonders, while those who trespass risk incurring the wrath of the dragon god.
subtitle: The Dragon King's Underwater Palace

keyMyths.title: Urashima Taro
keyMyths.description: Urashima Taro, a fisherman, is invited to Ryugu-jo and spends what he believes to be a few days there. Upon returning to the surface, he discovers that centuries have passed.

keyMyths.title: The Jewels of Ryujin
keyMyths.description: Ryujin possesses magical jewels that control the tides, and these jewels are sometimes sought after by heroes or stolen by villains, leading to dramatic consequences.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation of the Kojiki, which contains early references to sea deities and possibly related concepts.
sources.text: Translation of the Kojiki, which contains early references to sea deities and possibly related concepts.

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough
sources.work: Tales of Ise: Lyrical Episodes from Tenth-Century Japan
sources.description: Provides insight into the cultural context of stories related to magical realms.
sources.text: Provides insight into the cultural context of stories related to magical realms.
